aliases: Paneer and Vegetable Dumplings in Cream Sauce
Cottage Cheese Kofta in Almond Cream Gravy

english: Paneer and Vegetable Dumplings in Cream Sauce
Cottage Cheese Kofta in Almond Cream Gravy

food_subgroup: Vegetarian curry with fried dumplings in cream-based gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Shahi Malai Kofta. Mapped to closest parent 'curry (food product)' FOODON_03315595 as this is a North Indian cream-based curry dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Shahi Malai Kofta. Mapped to A00GJ (Dishes based on vegetables) as the kofta dumplings are primarily vegetable and paneer-based served in a cream curry sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Shahi Malai Kofta or its closest parent category in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Shahi Malai Kofta is approximately 300 g, comprising 4–6 fried paneer-vegetable dumplings in a generous portion of creamy almond-cashew gravy, usually served alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Malai Kofta / Paneer Kofta in cream gravy — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) component data: paneer, mixed vegetables, cream, almond, cashew, and cooking oil
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Shahi Malai Kofta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data for paneer (~25% of kofta mass), mixed vegetables (~20%), cream/malai (~15%), almond-cashew paste (~10%), cooking oil for frying (~10%), and tomato-onion gravy base (~20%). Energy and macros cross-checked against published Indian restaurant food composition studies (Longvah et al., 2017, ICMR-NIN). Values represent a moderate-richness restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and paneer curry dishes — analogous estimate based on GI values for paneer-based curry (GI ~40–50) and vegetable curry (GI ~45–55) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Shahi Malai Kofta. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous paneer-vegetable curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 40–55 for similar cream-based Indian curries with moderate carbohydrate content from vegetables and nut pastes). The cream and fat content further attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 33.0) / 100 = 14.9 for a 300 g serving (11.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3 = 33.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Shahi Malai Kofta to small portions (≤150 g) due to its moderate-to-high sodium content from added salt and spice blends, and should request reduced-salt preparation when ordering from restaurants.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~14.9 per restaurant serving) and high fat content from cream and nut pastes
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Shahi Malai Kofta in controlled portions (≤150 g) given its low GI (~45) and moderate glycemic load, but should account for the high caloric density from cream and nut pastes and pair with low-GI accompaniments such as roti rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~6.8 g per 100 g) from cream, paneer, and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Shahi Malai Kofta to occasional consumption due to its elevated saturated fat content from dairy cream and paneer, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; requesting preparation with reduced cream or substituting with low-fat yoghurt is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shahi Malai Kofta is a rich North Indian Mughal-inspired vegetarian curry dish consisting of soft, deep-fried dumplings (kofta) made from paneer (Indian cottage cheese), mashed potato, and mixed vegetables, simmered in a luxurious cream-based gravy enriched with almond paste, cashew paste, and aromatic whole spices including cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon. The name 'Shahi' means 'royal' in Hindi, reflecting the dish's Mughal court origins, while 'Malai' refers to the cream used in the sauce. The kofta are typically golden-brown on the outside with a soft, mildly spiced interior, and the gravy is velvety, mildly sweet, and subtly nutty with a warm spice profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 185 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 13.5 g fat (of which 6.8 g is saturated), and 11.0 g carbohydrate, making it calorie-dense due to the cream and nut paste content. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), attenuated by the high fat and protein content. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (sodium ~320 mg/100 g), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate glycemic load per serving), and cardiovascular disease (elevated saturated fat). It is suitable for vegetarians and can be halal-certified depending on preparation. Shahi Malai Kofta is commonly served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Bombay Grill, and is typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice.

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Shahi Malai Kofta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data for the constituent ingredients (paneer, cream, vegetables, nut pastes, oil) and cross-checked against published Indian restaurant food composition literature. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar paneer-vegetable cream curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level ICMR-NIN data is authoritative and the estimation methodology is systematic and documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data; no single composite entry for Shahi Malai Kofta exists in any approved primary source. Values should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available in ICMR-NIN or HPB databases. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN component data for this dish type and is not reliably present in the constituent ingredients at measurable levels. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 tables for the constituent ingredients used in this estimation. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting trace levels from dairy fat (paneer, cream) rather than industrial trans fats. 6. The dish is served at Bombay Grill (Restaurant Delivery) on field survey data at SGD 16.90. 7. Halal status is listed as true based on the vegetarian nature of the dish; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data and should be treated as indicative rather than analytically confirmed values.
